say_this: I'm going to change your diaper now. I'm lifting your legs — up they go. Feel that? Cool wipe. And here's the clean one. One snap, two snaps — all done. You're all fresh.
do_this: Narrate each step as you do it — name body parts, objects, sensations
Let baby kick freely with the diaper off for a minute
Pause before picking baby up — tell them what is about to happen
Let baby hold the clean diaper or pull at their shirt
what_this_builds: Language through narration, body awareness, the experience of being a participant in their own care

say_this: Time to eat! Here's one piece of banana, two pieces. It's soft and yellow. Can you pick it up? You got it! That's a big bite. Want more? One more piece.
do_this: Let baby self-feed with finger foods — the mess is the learning
Name foods, colors, textures, and temperatures as you serve them
Count food pieces as you put them on the tray
Start and end every meal the same way: bib on, sit down, eat, clean up, bib off
what_this_builds: Self-feeding skills, food vocabulary, counting in context, the concept of mealtimes as structured routine

say_this: Tummy time! Can you push up? Look how strong you are. Can you reach that toy? Stretch! You're working so hard. It's right there. You almost got it.
do_this: Get down on the floor at baby's eye level
Place a toy just out of easy reach — close enough to be possible
Do not rescue immediately if baby fusses — effort-fussing is not distress
Narrate their movements: "You're lifting your head! You're reaching!"
what_this_builds: Neck, shoulder, and core strength, frustration tolerance, the experience of effort leading to reward
post_at: Main play area or nursery floor

say_this: Which one do you want? You pick. Oh, you chose the rattle! Shake it — hear that sound? Now try this one. It's different. Heavy. Can you pass it to your other hand?
do_this: Lay out several objects and let baby choose — do not direct their play
Offer objects of different sizes, textures, and weights
When baby drops something, wait before picking it up — let them figure out where it went
Step back and observe before engaging — follow their lead
what_this_builds: Grasping, reaching, object exploration, self-directed play, the habit of choosing for themselves

say_this: Feel that wind! It's cool today. Listen — hear the birds? I see a big tree. And a little flower. Look up — the sky is blue. Feel the grass. It's soft and a little wet.
do_this: Go outside every day, in every weather that is safe
Let baby touch natural things: grass, bark, leaves, stones, dirt
Name what you see, hear, and feel — build vocabulary for the physical world
Count things you pass: birds, trees, cars, flowers
what_this_builds: Sensory awareness of the natural world, environmental vocabulary, the foundation of observation

say_this: Bath time! Feel the warm water. Let's wash your tummy. Now your arms. Splash! You made a splash. Can you kick? Big kicks! Let's count your toes — one, two, three, four, five.
do_this: Name body parts as you wash them
Let baby kick, splash, and move freely in the water
Use cups for pouring — narrate "full" and "empty"
Let baby hold the washcloth and try to "wash" themselves
what_this_builds: Body part vocabulary, water awareness, motor practice against resistance, self-care participation

say_this: Pajamas on. Now our book. Then our song. Then sleep. Same as always. You know this. I love you. Goodnight. See you in the morning.
do_this: Follow the exact same sequence every night: pajamas, book, song, goodnight words, lights off
Do not skip steps — shorten if needed, but keep the order
Use the same closing words every single night
If baby fusses, stay calm and stay with the routine — the routine is the soothing
what_this_builds: The most important routine of the day, the nervous system's ability to anticipate sleep, trust that endings are safe
post_at: Nursery wall near crib or rocker

say_this: Let's read! Look at this picture — that's a bear. A big brown bear. Can you touch the bear? And what's on this page? One, two, three stars. Can you help turn the page? Pull!
do_this: Hold baby in your lap with the book where they can see and touch it
Point to pictures and name them — pause on each page
Let baby turn pages, grab at pictures, mouth the book corners
Read at least one book a day — even on the worst days, even if it is the same book again
what_this_builds: The habit of reading, print awareness, vocabulary, the association between books and closeness and safety
closing_line: You are not reading to a baby who does not understand. You are building a brain that will.

say_this: Time to change your diaper. Can you walk here? Thank you. Can you pull your pants down? Pull — you're doing it. I'm going to take this one off. It's wet. Now a clean, dry one. Can you pull your pants back up? Up, up — you did it. All clean.
do_this: Let them walk to the changing area (not carry them)
Ask them to pull pants down and up — help only after they try
Narrate each step: "I'm lifting, I'm wiping, here's the clean one"
Count toes or name body parts while changing
what_this_builds: Body awareness, participation in self-care, motor independence, language through narration.

say_this: Time to get dressed. Do you want the red shirt or the blue shirt? You picked blue! Can you put it on? Pull it over your head. Arms through — one arm, two arms. Now pants. Step in — one leg, two legs. Pull up! You got dressed.
do_this: Offer two choices — both acceptable. They decide.
Let them dress themselves — accept backwards and inside-out
Name colors and count items: "One sock, two socks"
Talk them through the motions: "Grab, pull, push, step"
Only help after they try and ask (or show real frustration)
what_this_builds: Decision-making, fine motor progression, self-dressing identity, color and counting practice.

say_this: What's on your plate? Let's name everything. Can you use your fork to poke the chicken? Try scooping the rice with your spoon. Do you want more? Say 'more, please.' Thank you! When you're all done, carry your plate to the counter.
do_this: Name every food: color, texture, taste, temperature
Utensil practice at every meal — expect mess, do not take over
Count food items: "One, two, three strawberries"
Model and expect "please" and "thank you" at every exchange
Child carries own plate to counter after every meal
If they spill, hand them a cloth — they clean it
what_this_builds: Vocabulary, self-feeding, counting, social language, contribution to the meal routine.

say_this: Let's go outside! What's the weather? Feel the air — warm or cold? Look at that tree. Touch the bark — rough! Can you run to the fence? Now let's count the rocks. One, two, three. What else do you see? A bird! Where did it fly?
do_this: Check weather together before going out. Name it.
Touch everything safe: bark, leaves, rocks, grass, mud, water
Name what you see, hear, smell, feel
Run, jump, kick, climb, carry — let them move
Count natural objects. Compare sizes. Find shapes.
Let them lead the walk and choose the path
what_this_builds: Environmental awareness, nature vocabulary, gross motor confidence, sensory engagement, counting in context.

say_this: What do you want to play with? You choose. Show me what you're making. A tower! How many blocks is that? One, two, three, four. Four blocks tall! Uh oh, it fell. That's frustrating. Do you want to try again? You're figuring it out.
do_this: Let them choose the activity and lead the play
Narrate what they do, but do not direct it
Count, sort, name colors during play — weave it in naturally
When they struggle, wait 30 seconds before helping
Name their emotions: "You're frustrated," "You're proud"
Cleanup before switching to the next activity — every time
what_this_builds: Self-directed play, problem-solving, frustration tolerance, math and language through natural play.

say_this: Bath time! Can you undress yourself? Now climb in — hold the side. Feel the water — warm! Here's the soap. Can you wash your arms? Rub, rub, rub. Now squeeze the sponge — squeeze hard! Let's pour water. How many pours to fill the cup? One, two, three — full!
do_this: Let them undress and climb in (with spotting)
They wash their own body — you do hair and missed spots
Pour, squeeze, splash — fine motor and sensory exploration
Count pours, name body parts, describe temperatures
Explore sink/float with different objects
They climb out and try to dry themselves
what_this_builds: Self-care independence, sensory vocabulary, fine motor (pouring, squeezing), body-part naming, water physics.

say_this: Bedtime routine! What's first? Pajamas — you put them on. Now teeth — you brush first, I'll finish. Now one book. You pick. Let's read. What do you see on this page? Good night. I love you. Same thing tomorrow.
do_this: Same sequence every night — no variation, no negotiation
They do pajamas (you help with hard parts)
They brush teeth first (you finish)
They choose the book (from two options or from the shelf)
They turn pages during read-aloud
Same closing words every single night
what_this_builds: The non-negotiable bedtime anchor, self-care progression, reading engagement, emotional security through predictability.

say_this: Let's look at this page. What do you see? A cat! What color is the cat? Orange! How many birds are on the tree? One, two — two birds. How does this character feel? She looks happy. Why do you think she's happy? What do you think happens next?
do_this: Point and name. Then ask them to point and name.
Count objects on each page
Ask "how does he/she feel?" — build empathy through story
On familiar books, pause and let them fill in the word
Ask open-ended questions: "What do you see?" not "Is that a dog?"
Let them hold the book and turn pages
what_this_builds: Vocabulary explosion, narrative engagement, counting, emotion recognition, prediction, the habit of reading.

say_this: We're almost done. In two minutes, it's time to clean up. Okay — time. Put the blocks in the box. You do it. I know you want to keep playing. I understand. We clean up before we start the next thing. Thank you for helping. The floor is clear. What's next?
do_this: Give a 2-minute warning before every transition
They clean up — you can help, but they do the majority
"First ___, then ___" language for every transition
Name their feelings about stopping: "I know you're disappointed"
Hold the rule: nothing new starts until the old thing is put away
Praise the effort: "You cleaned your own space."
what_this_builds: Transition tolerance, task completion, routine ownership, emotional flexibility, the habit of finishing before starting.

say_this: You're really upset. I see that. You're angry because [name the reason]. I understand. That IS hard. I'm right here. You're safe. When you're ready, we can [next step]. The answer is still [the limit]. I love you and the answer is still [the limit].
do_this: Stay calm. Your calm is the lesson. Breathe.
Name the emotion: "You're angry." "You're frustrated." "You're disappointed."
Validate the feeling: "I understand. That IS hard."
Hold the limit: Do not cave. Do not negotiate. Do not lecture.
Stay physically present. Get low. Do not tower over them.
Do not say "calm down." Do not say "you're okay." (They are not okay — name what is true.)
Wait. It will pass. When it does: "That was a big feeling. You got through it."
Then return to whatever the routine requires — the meltdown does not cancel the expectation.
what_this_builds: Emotional regulation through co-regulation, the knowledge that feelings are survivable, trust that the adult will stay calm and present, the separation of emotion from outcome.
closing_line: Print and post where the action happens. Replace when worn.

say_this: Check the weather. What do you need to wear? Go to your drawer, pick everything out, and get dressed. The whole thing — shirt, pants, socks, shoes. Come find me when you're completely ready. If something is tricky, try it yourself first.
do_this: Child checks weather and decides what to wear independently
Child retrieves and puts on all clothing without help (except laces)
If something is backwards, ask "Does that feel right?" before fixing
Do not enter the room — let them manage the full sequence alone
what_this_builds: Self-care independence, fine motor (fasteners), decision-making, self-assessment

say_this: How many people are eating? Get that many plates, forks, and napkins. Pour your own drink. After you eat, clear your plate, wipe your spot, push in your chair. Tell me about your day while we eat — what happened first? Then what?
do_this: Child sets the table using one-to-one correspondence (one item per person)
Child pours own drink from a small pitcher and serves self
Conversation at the table: narration of the day, storytelling, vocabulary expansion
Child clears own place completely after eating — no reminders
what_this_builds: Practical life competence, one-to-one correspondence, narrative language, routine ownership

say_this: What do you see? Describe it to me — use three words. Why do you think that bird is doing that? Let's draw what we find. Look closely at this leaf — what do you notice about it? How is it different from that one?
do_this: Spend at least one hour outside. Nature observation, physical play, or nature journaling
Ask open-ended questions: "What do you notice? Why do you think? What's different?"
Nature journaling: draw one thing from observation — focus on detail, not artistic quality
Collect, count, sort, compare: sticks by length, rocks by size, leaves by shape
what_this_builds: Observation skills, descriptive vocabulary, scientific curiosity, gross motor confidence

say_this: Tell me about what you're building. What's the story? ... That's interesting. What happens next? ... If it's hard, try it yourself first. I'll help if you really need it, but give it a try. ... When you're done, clean up before you start the next thing.
do_this: Ask your child to narrate their play — who, what, where, why
When they encounter difficulty, wait before helping. Ask: "What could you try?"
Cleanup between activities: non-negotiable, with decreasing reminders
Notice sustained engagement: 15-20 minutes on one activity is excellent
what_this_builds: Narrative language, problem-solving, sustained attention, cleanup habit

say_this: What's your chore? When does it need to be done? I'm not going to remind you — you own this. When it's done, come tell me. If you're not sure how, try it first. If you really need help, ask.
do_this: Child has one owned chore: they notice it needs doing and do it without prompting
Real contribution: setting table, watering plants, feeding pets, wiping surfaces, sweeping
Emphasize completion: start to finish, including putting away any tools used
When finished, child reports: "I'm done." This is the beginning of accountability
what_this_builds: Initiative, task ownership, accountability, real-world contribution

say_this: Do the whole bath yourself. Wash body, wash hair, rinse. Tell me about your day while you're in there — what made you happy? What was hard? What are you proud of? When you're done, dry off, hang up the towel, pajamas on.
do_this: Child manages entire bath independently (parent present for safety)
Use bath time for daily emotional check-in: happy, hard, proud
Introduce feeling words beyond basics: proud, disappointed, frustrated, grateful, nervous
Full post-bath routine: dry, towel hung, pajamas, teeth brushed — all independent
what_this_builds: Self-care mastery, emotional vocabulary, reflective habit, full independence

say_this: What's the bedtime routine? Tell me the steps. ... Good. Now do them. Pajamas, teeth, pick a book, get in bed. Same every night. No negotiations. When I come in, you should be ready.
do_this: Child states the bedtime routine before executing it
Child manages the physical preparation independently: pajamas, teeth, book selection
The routine is the same every night — consistency is the mechanism
If the child resists or stalls, name-validate-hold: "I know you don't want to go to bed. Bedtime is bedtime."
what_this_builds: Routine ownership, consistency, self-directed preparation, emotional regulation during transitions

say_this: Look at the cover. What do you think this story is about? ... Let's find out. [Read.] ... Now tell it to me. What happened at the beginning? Then what? How did it end? ... Was there a part you liked best? Why? ... What would you have done if you were that character?
do_this: Read aloud 2-3 times per day. At least one should be followed by narration
After reading, ask: "Now you tell me the story." Accept whatever they give
Do not correct or quiz. If the retelling is short, ask one question: "What else happened?"
Over weeks, narrations will naturally grow longer and more detailed. Track the trend
what_this_builds: Listening comprehension, narrative retelling, story structure, vocabulary, the narration habit

say_this: We're switching. What do you need to do first? ... Right — clean up. Everything back where it belongs. I'm not going to remind you again. When it's done, we move on.
do_this: One warning, one time call, then expect action
Child identifies what needs to be cleaned up and where it goes
No repeated reminders. If they stall: "The next activity starts when cleanup is finished. I'll wait."
When they transition cleanly, name it: "You cleaned up and moved on without a single reminder."
what_this_builds: Transition management, cleanup habit, self-regulation, response to time cues

say_this: You're really [angry/frustrated/disappointed]. I can see that. It's okay to feel that way. It is not okay to [hit/throw/scream]. You can say 'I'm angry.' You can take a deep breath. When you're ready, we'll figure out what to do next. I'm right here.
do_this: Name the emotion. Validate it. Hold the boundary. Do not cave, lecture, or punish
Stay calm. Your regulation is the model — they are borrowing your nervous system
Wait. Do not rush recovery. They need to feel the feeling and come through the other side
After recovery, reconnect: "You were really upset. You calmed down. That's hard to do. What happened?"
what_this_builds: Emotional regulation, recovery from distress, the separation of feeling from action, resilience
closing_line: Print, tear out, and post where the action happens.
